data.title: PGJ: One Of The Less-Appealing Chinese ETFs
data.description:
What's Different About This Chinese ETF?

The Invesco Golden Dragon China ETF (PGJ), which has been around for over two decades now, is a passively managed product (it follows full replication, rather than sampling, in its efforts to track its index) that seeks to track the Nasdaq Golden Dragon China Index [NGDCI]. Perhaps one of the first questions that prospective investors may have is - " How is PGJ different from other Chinese ETFs?"

Well, PGJ and its tracking index stand out because it only focuses on "US-listed" stocks from the PRC [People's Republic of China]. To get more specific, all constituents who make the cut are required to be listed on one of these US-listed exchanges - The Nasdaq Stock Market, the New York Stock Exchange, NYSE American or Cboe BZX Exchange, and not on exchanges from Hong Kong, Mainland China, or anywhere else. Note that these businesses are still all headquartered or incorporated in the PRC and are also required to generate the majority of their revenue from that region. Given the US-listed requirements, one is bound to see a plethora of ADRs [American Depositary Receipts].

To get some perspective, investors may peruse PGJ's top 10 holdings (the ETF currently covers 79 stocks in total); with the exception of 1 holding- Yum China Holdings, Inc (YUMC), all the others are ADRs. For the uninitiated, YUMC may be incorporated in Delaware, but it is still also the largest restaurant company in China with " substantially" all of the company's revenue and long-lived assets based in the latter.

The obvious benefit of a PGJ is that you get the superior liquidity and transparency of US markets, but the drawback is that since PGJ relies on US listed stocks, their reaction time will take place with a lag, and won't necessarily be on sync with what's happening in China. Also, if geopolitical tensions were to materially worsen, these holdings could be susceptible to delisting from the US

Coming back to NGDCI, even though it tilts its holdings on the basis of their market-cap, it also adopts a unique weighting procedure, where the top 5 stocks are only allowed to have a maximum weight of 8%; for all the other constituents, the maximum weight is capped at a lower threshold of 4%. Put another way, only 5 stocks from this portfolio are permitted to have an individual weight of over 4%, and the maximum individual stock weight you will see in this portfolio is 8% (at the time of rebalancing which takes place four time in year- March, June, September, December). Given this quarterly capping policy, one can expect the likes of Baidu (BIDU), Alibaba (BABA), and YUMC to see their stakes being cut in March, as they are all above 8% at this juncture.

How Does PGJ Compare To MCHI?

Retail investors who are looking for diversified and liquid exposure to Chinese stocks are typically directed to the iShares MSCI China ETF (MCHI), which passively covers the famed MSCI China Index (an index used by institutions to benchmark their Chinese and Asian portfolios).

MCHI's clout can be measured through its mammoth AUM and liquidity it sees every day; to elaborate, even though MCHI made it to the markets only +6 years after PGJ, it has still garnered AUM of over $8000M, which is a whopping 60x higher. Also take note of the average daily volumes; while PGJ can barely even procure a million dollars' worth of volume per day, MCHI is managing over 150M per day. What this also means is that you will find PGJ encounter some rather wide spreads between its bid and ask (in percentage terms it works out to 0.17%, whereas MCHI's is very tight at just 0.01%), making it unsuitable for taking short-term bets.

MCHI is also broader in scope, covering a pool of stocks that is 7x more than PGJ's narrow portfolio. However, note that MCHI still tilts towards the giant-cap stocks in China (average market-cap of $92B as opposed to PGJ, which is more large-cap centric).

With regards to most other structural aspects, PGJ comes across as inferior. In fairness, PGJ has a cheaper management expense fee than MCHI (0.5% as opposed to 0.59% for MCHI), but then it also has another "other expense" component amounting to 0.2% of fees taking total fees to a higher threshold of 0.7%.

PGJ is also more prone to churn (annual churn that is double that of MCHI) prompting more transaction costs, and also sees higher volatility (which makes it a riskier bet). The only area where PGJ has an edge is the dividend front, where it pays more frequently, with the annualized figure working out to 3.29%, which is an impressive 126 bps more than MCHI's figure.

In terms of performance, there's been quite a tug-of-war between these two products since MCHI got listed, with both products outperforming at different stages over the last 15 years or so, but in aggregate terms, it is MCHI that is currently leading PGJ by quite a hefty margin of 1.7x. Investors may also want to consider that PGJ does not really have a great track record of "risk-adjusted" returns across the long-term or short-term, with its Sharpe ratio readings lagging that of MCHI.

Is PGJ ETF Worth Pursuing Now?

As implied in the previous section, with the exception of its dividend profile, we don't view PGJ as a very compelling Chinese-focused product, and thus find it difficult to recommend it.

Even if you still want to overlook what we've discussed above, we are not sure China, and its valuations look particularly alluring at this juncture.

It's fair to say that if PGJ is to do well, a lot will hinge on how well its discretionary component fares, as that sector alone accounts for half the portfolio. It is questionable if demand conditions are solid enough for these firms to flourish, as retail sales in China are growing at a meager pace of less than 1%, which is at 3 year lows.

Consumer confidence readings have picked up sequentially, and that is to be expected given the stimulus (that includes a sub $9B consumer goods trade-in scheme) boost and the onset of the lunar New Year, but investors should be mindful that these latest figures are still at quite a low threshold and well below the long-term mean of 108.

Then from a valuation angle, PGJ's portfolio of Chinese stocks isn't necessarily the cheapest option around. To elaborate, PGJ is now priced at a higher earnings multiple of 14.4 and a higher cash flow multiple of 10.3x than a diversified basket of emerging market stocks.

We also feel it would be unwise to pursue PGJ now, when there is a dearth of momentum in its counter; note that it is currently trading below all its key moving averages, reflecting a lack of interest from the bulls.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Confluence Investment Management's Keller Q4 2025 Quarterly
data.description:
Happy 2026! While I'm in my fifth decade in the investment business, this is the first year in that spell that Warren Buffett is not the CEO of Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B). As you probably know, he stepped down from the helm of that company at the end of 2025. Mr. Buffett started the Buffett Partnership the year I was born, and then in 1969 liquidated his partnerships and transferred to his partners an interest in Berkshire Hathaway Inc., his new corporate vehicle. By the time I began working for A.G. Edwards & Sons in 1978, Mr. Buffett had written 10 letters to Berkshire shareholders. A few years later, I read a magazine article that said reading Mr. Buffett's shareholder letters was better than earning an MBA. So, I started reading those letters.

Reading Buffett's letters was like listening to my father, himself an accomplished and wise businessman. It was common sense. It was simple. Business isn't hard, but you can make it hard if you over-think it. What Mr. Buffett taught me was that stocks are just fractional ownership interests in businesses. So, to invest in stocks, you had better learn what makes one business better than another. That doesn't mean guessing which businesses will do better in the year ahead. It means determining what sort of businesses can do well year after year for decades, and why . Mr. Buffett's letters were a graduate-level education in why some businesses just perform better than others over time. Those letters have guided the Confluence team to a better understanding of investing. And if you have been investing with us for a long time, those letters have benefited you.

An old financial advisor at A.G. Edwards called me about 25 years ago to talk about investing. We had known each other for many years, and I always learned from his observations. This time he told me, "Ben Graham taught us to buy stocks cheap." (By the way, if you have not read Graham's The Intelligent Investor , you should.) The advisor went on to say, "Phil Fisher taught us to buy growth." (If you have not read Fisher's Common Stocks and Uncommon Profits , you should.) My friend concluded, "Warren Buffett taught us to buy growth cheap." That is successful investing in a nutshell.

As I tell all our new employees, this is an apprenticeship business. You not only learn by reading the words of successful practitioners, you learn by working with older colleagues, absorbing what they've learned. Two months ago, we bid farewell to a gentleman who was instrumental in teaching many of us at Confluence. Boyd Poston came to work in the A.G. Edwards research department in the early 1980s, after working a decade at another local investment organization. He was immediately one of the few senior members among mostly young analysts. A natural teacher (he taught the investment course at St. Louis University's business school for over 20 years), Boyd took us all under his wing and gladly imparted his wisdom.

When the opportunity arose to move to the buy-side and manage money at A.G. Edwards Asset Management, Boyd came along and established himself once again as an outstanding investor and teacher. Boyd's investment prowess contributed greatly to our performance at that predecessor firm to Confluence. He also tutored all the investment professionals there,

including many who are still part of the Confluence team today. Boyd retired in 2008, just before I departed to Confluence. He passed away this past November at the age of 86. We miss him greatly but fondly remember him as we recall his aphorisms. “Never invest in a company whose CEO has a deep tan,” he once remarked. “He’s not spending enough time at the office.” Boyd Poston and Warren Buffett thought alike.

A verity of investing is the passage of time. Another thing I learned from Messrs. Buffett and Poston is that for equity investors time is your friend . This, of course, runs counter to human nature, which always wants satisfaction as soon as possible. A great company is a great long-term investment, especially if it’s undervalued. But there is no rule that requires it to become valued more highly in the next 12 months. This can be tough for the average investor but can work to be a real advantage for the patient investor. Impatience is the enemy of successful investing. Patience is the greatest virtue. Quoting Benjamin Graham, “In the short run, the market is a voting machine, but in the long run, it is a weighing machine.” Investing at its most basic is really very simple.

We appreciate your confidence in us.

Gratefully,

Mark A. Keller, CFACEO and Chief Investment Officer

This letter was prepared by Mark Keller of Confluence Investment Management LLC and reflects the current opinion of the author. It is based upon sources and data believed to be accurate and reliable. Opinions and forward-looking statements expressed are subject to change. This information does not constitute a solicitation or an offer to buy or sell any security.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Journey Medical: Emrosi Is Winning, But The Stock Hasn't Noticed
data.description:
Most microcap biopharma stocks are binary bets. You either get a drug approval and a buyout or you get a failed trial and a zero. Journey Medical (DERM) is different, and I think the market is having a hard time categorising it. As opposed to a research house burning cash, it's a commercial operation that was already hovering around breakeven before its flagship product really started contributing.

The stock is trading around $9.30, giving it a market cap of roughly $295m. For that price, you get a stable portfolio of dermatology assets doing ~$55m in revenue. You also get Emrosi, a new oral rosacea drug that launched in April and is putting up numbers that suggest it could eventually be worth more than the entire EV.

The Medic Model

The core of the bull case relies on management's pedigree, specifically CEO Claude Maraoui, who held multiple leadership roles at Medicis, the dermatology giant sold to Valeant/Bausch for >$2.5bn. The broader team is similarly composed of veterans of the dermatology market who know this specific niche well.

They built a base business of alternative or niche products (e.g. Qbrexza for hyperhidrosis, Accutane for acne, etc.) to pay the bills and keep a sales force of ~35 reps active. This is important because it means the launch of Emrosi didn't require building a commercial infrastructure from scratch. The reps were already visiting the same doctors who write prescriptions for rosacea treatment.

This setup creates an interesting floor. Even if Emrosi was a failure (which the data suggests it isn't), the company has enough cash flow from the base portfolio to keep the lights on. There isn't the typical dilution spiral of a pre-revenue biotech.

A Better Alternative

For 20 years, Oracea (doxycycline) has been the default oral treatment for rosacea. It generates over $300m a year despite being just okay in terms of efficacy.

Emrosi (minocycline) is more than just an alternative, it actually beat out Oracea in Phase 3 trials:

  • Clearance: 62.7% of Emrosi patients achieved treatment success vs. 39% for Oracea.

  • Lesion count: Emrosi reduced lesion counts by 19 vs. 14.8 for Oracea.

  • Speed: It worked faster, separating from placebo as early as Week 2.

Getting doctors to switch to a new treatment is challenging, but they will do so if a drug makes their patients happier faster. The clinical data says Emrosi does exactly that. The safety profile is also comparable to placebo, which removes the main hesitation a doctor might have in switching patients off a legacy drug.

The Launch Is Working

We don't need to guess if the commercial strategy is viable anymore. The drug launched in Apr-25 and by Oct-25 they had filled nearly 34,000 prescriptions.

In Q3 alone, Emrosi generated $4.9m in net sales. That is an annualised run rate of nearly $20m essentially right out of the gate. More importantly, the prescriber base is widening. Over 2,700 unique providers have written a script, which indicates that Emrosi is seeing broad adoption.

Commercial execution is also de-risking the story. Coverage has ramped from 54m lives in May-25 to >100m lives by July-25. This is a key threshold that removes the primary source of friction for prescribers and ensures that the demand generated by sales reps can actually be fulfilled by pharmacies.

Pricing in a Flop

When you strip out the noise, there is a clear disconnect in the valuation. With a market cap of roughly $295m and a net cash position of roughly zero (cash cancels out debt), the EV also sits around $295m.

The legacy dermatology business is stable and generates about $55m in revenue. Healthy specialty pharma peers, such as Collegium Pharmaceutical (COLL) and ANI Pharmaceuticals (ANIP), trade at ~2x sales. Using a conservative 1.5x multiple, the base business is worth roughly $83m.

Subtract that from the EV, and the market is effectively valuing Emrosi, a drug that just beat the standard of care head-to-head, at around $212m.

That implies the market is pricing Emrosi at just over 1x its estimated peak sales ($200m). This is where the opportunity lies, as high-margin dermatology assets with patent protection often trade at 3–4x sales. Arcutis Biotherapeutics (ARQT), which is currently ramping a new dermatology drug, and Galderma both trade at over 9x sales.

At a modest 3x sales on $100m revenue (half the target), Emrosi alone would be worth $300m. At 3x sales on the full $200m target, the drug is worth $600m.

In either scenario, the current implied valuation of ~$212m offers a significant margin of safety. If they hit the $200m target, we are looking at a stock that is primed for a significant re-rating. The operating leverage is notable because the commercial infrastructure is already paid for. The same 35 reps selling the old drugs will now be selling the new one, meaning every new Emrosi prescription drops to the bottom line, pushing gross margins (already ~67%) even higher.

Risks to the Thesis

The biggest risk I see is stalling sales. Better drugs have failed in the past because sales reps couldn't get access or payors put up too many hurdles. While early data is good, Q4-25 and Q1-26 need to show continued ramp up. If prescriptions plateau at 3000 per month, the thesis breaks.

There is also the threat from generics. Patents are listed until 2039, but generic manufacturers are likely to file challenges around 2027. Litigation is expensive and introduces uncertainty even if Journey is likely to win given the specific formulation patents.

Finally, relying on a single growth engine is always risky. If a new safety signal pops up, it could kill the momentum. However, since the Phase 3 data showed safety comparable to placebo, this concern is mitigated significantly.

Bottom Line

It all comes down to execution. The drug works, the launch data is tracking well, and the valuation hasn't caught up. At $9.30, I think the downside is protected by the base business while the upside from Emrosi is essentially a free option. I expect the next few earnings calls to force a re-rating as the revenue ramp becomes undeniable.

data.title: ETO: Tax-Efficient Monthly Income From Global Equities
data.description:
Overview

While market indices continue to hover within range of their all-time highs, it can be difficult to locate attractive opportunities in the market. However, a closed-end fund like the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund (ETO) continues to trade at a deeply discounted price to NAV valuation, which I believe may be attributed to the fund's use of leverage. Therefore, I wanted to revisit the fund to assess its current value proposition, latest annual report, and risks at this time. When I previously covered ETO, I issued a buy rating, and the fund has outperformed the S&P 500 Index (SPX) since then.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that ETO's share price has increased by 15.4%. The fund continues to demonstrate its ability to ride the positive market momentum over the last few quarters. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 23.3% over the same time frame. ETO now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 7% and issues payouts on a monthly basis. I believe that one of the strongest aspects of ETO is the tax-efficiency and flexibility that the distributions can provide investors.

Additionally, I wanted to revisit the fund to discuss the strong dividend history. The fund has maintained a consistent distribution range, which allows long-term investors to grow their annual dividend income over time by reinvesting distributions or consistently adding new capital to their position. However, I also wanted to revisit the fund's tax efficiency, which is a great benefit for investors that want to limit their overall tax consequences within their portfolio. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that ETO implements to generate its earnings for shareholders.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, ETO has total net assets of ~$521M that is spread across a blended portfolio of equities and fixed income securities. The fund has a listed management fee of 1.03% of assets, which may be worth it considering the active management of positions and the high dividend yield that is offered. The fund's primary objective is to provide attractive total returns through its portfolio of global dividend-paying common and preferred stocks. This blended approach enables the fund to capture price upside and generate an amplified level of income within its portfolio. The portfolio's breakdown by asset type is as follows:

  • Equity: 83.69%
    • U.S. Equity: 47.83%
    • Foreign Equity: 35.85%
  • Fixed Income/Preferred: 14.81%

Expanding on this diversity, the fund maintains an efficient allocation across different sectors. As we can see below, ETO is most heavily allocated to financials at 27.39% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to information technology and consumer discretionary investments, which account for 20.65% and 9.62% of assets respectively. Rather than replicate any specific index, ETO's management handpicks what it believes to be the highest quality dividend-paying companies.

As a result, we can see that ETO provides direct equity exposure to some of the highest quality growth companies in the world. For instance, the largest individual position is in Alphabet (GOOG) at 5.35% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to many other notable names, including Nvidia (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), and ASML Holdings (ASML), just to name a few. The fund has exposure to about 180 holdings, but the top ten positions make up more than a fourth of total assets.

Updated Valuation & Annual Report

Since ETO is structured as a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of its underlying assets. As a result, ETO now trades at a discount to NAV of 7.55%. For reference, ETO has traded at an average discount to NAV of 5.55% over the last five years. Since the price currently trades at a larger-than-average discount to NAV, this can be an indication that it is an attractive time for long-term investors to accumulate shares. However, I believe it is more important to reference the red line on the graph below, which shows the current price to NAV discount in relation to its ten-year historical range.

However, I think there is a very straightforward explanation why the fund continues to trade at an attractive valuation compared to its last ten years. As we can see, the fund's price to NAV discount significantly increased throughout 2022, which is coincidentally when interest rates were aggressively raised to the highest level seen over the last ten years. According to the latest annual report for 2025, the fund utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify its earnings. The use of leverage currently amounts to 16.84% of total assets.

Although this is a moderate use of leverage, it introduces a sensitivity to interest rates due to the higher cost of debt. As interest rates remain elevated, the cost of debt rises, which negatively impacts the fund's operating spread between the cost of its debt and the yield collected from securities. To confirm this, we can review the internal performance of the fund over the last few years. Net investment income amounted to $0.65 per share, while net realized gains landed at $4.73 per share. However, net investment income amounted to $0.85 per share back in 2023, which may have been a period when the fund's spread was much healthier and the fixed income securities did not feel the full weight of higher rates yet.

The annual report also indicates that the net investment income levels aren't strong enough to fully support the distributions that the fund pays. This results in an underlying reliance on net realized gains to bridge the gap in earnings. The challenge is that this creates a vulnerability against market declines and if the fund cannot capture positive net realized gains, ETO will suffer large NAV declines during periods where markets are trending downward. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts over the last few years:

  • 2021: $31.37 per share
  • 2022: $21.99 per share
  • 2023: $22.69 per share
  • 2024: $27.73 per share
  • 2025: $31.03 per share

Risks

So although the fund provides direct exposure to some of the highest quality growth companies in the world and also has a high dividend yield, this doesn't automatically translate to higher returns over time. As the annual report reveals, ETO is heavily dependent on positive net realized gains to fuel its growth. This has two effects on the fund:

  1. ETO will have to sell off the upside growth of its strongest holdings, which interrupts capital appreciation potential.
  2. The fund offers very little resilience against declining markets.

Just to display this weakness, we can reference the fund's performance over the last decade. As we can see below, ETO's share price has increased by about 64.5% over the last decade. While this is solid, the fund's capital appreciation severely underperforms against something like the Vanguard Total World Stock Index Fund ETF (VT) over the same time frame. However, the inclusion of ETO's distributions results in outperformance against VT. Therefore, investors should anticipate that ~70% of ETO's total return will come from the distributions, while the remaining ~30% comes from capital appreciation.

While the fund has a strong history through the bull markets of the last decade, ETO's resilience during market downturns has not been as strong. A good example of this was demonstrated throughout the course of 2022's market decline, which can be attributed to uncertainty around the pandemic, rapid hikes to interest rates, and record-breaking inflationary data. As we can see below, ETO still suffered from a total loss of nearly 30%, despite having a large dividend yield. The fund is essentially paying out more than it earns, which exacerbates the decline in share price compared to a more traditional ETF like VT.

So as global uncertainty and tension rise due to the potential Greenland conflict, there can be a rotation in the market. Since ETO is heavily allocated to technology stocks, the fund may not be able to offer any resilience against the potential decline in the near-term. If the fund faces a scenario where its internal earnings struggle to keep up with distributions, the underlying NAV may decline and erode investor capital.

Tax-Efficient Dividends

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1733 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7%. Looking at the history over the last ten years, ETO has maintained a healthy track record of keeping distribution steady. Although payouts were adjusted throughout 2020 and 2022, ETO has been able to raise payouts back to its prior levels when earnings were strong. Despite there not being a history of traditional dividend raises over time, I believe the consistency should still be commended because it allows long-term investors to create their own growth. This can be achieved by reinvesting distributions into accumulating more shares, or by adding new capital on a consistent basis.

To demonstrate this concept, I ran a back test of an original $100,000 investment in ETO at the start of 2016. This calculation assumes that no additional capital was ever added after the initial $100K. This also assumes that all distributions received were reinvested back into the accumulation of more shares. By simply remaining invested and reinvesting dividends, we can see how an investor's annual income would have significantly improved year over year.

2016 Dividend Income: $9,692

2026: Dividend Income: $21,444

However, I believe a stronger aspect of ETO's appeal is the tax-efficiency of the fund. For reference, the latest section 19(a) notice indicates that 85.9% of the distributions paid YTD were supported by net realized long-term capital gains, which are considered to be qualified dividends and have the most favorable tax consequences for investors. The remaining 13.5% of distributions paid YTD were supported by net investment income, which has the least favorable tax treatment. So although 'tax-efficient' doesn't automatically mean tax-free, it still offers flexibility for investors that want to minimize their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that ETO is still a buy at this time due to the large discount to NAV valuation. However, the caveat is that the discounted valuation may be related to the fact that the fund continues to utilize leverage in this high-interest rate environment. As interest rates remain elevated, the underlying operating spread between the cost of its debt and the yield collected from securities is thinner. So while future interest rate cuts can be a catalyst for growth, we don't know how aggressive future interest rate cuts will be. Additionally, the fund may be vulnerable to any escalation in global uncertainty or conflict. After reviewing the latest annual report for 2025, the fund leans heavily on its ability to capture positive net realized gains, which can be a problem if markets experience a prolonged decline.

data.title: Technology Is Losing Momentum, But FTEC Does Not Lose My Interest
data.description:
If I think of an indexed info tech ETF, the Fidelity MSCI Information Technology Index ETF (FTEC) seems to confirm itself as a choice for indexed exposure. It is a very “hot” topic, where analysts proceed with great caution. The reason? Relative forward valuations of info tech show fluctuations that leave room for divergent interpretations, often in contradiction with one another.

Here I’ll tell you my view, even though I recognize it is quite contrarian: I do not think that the tech sector has unsustainable valuations. Considering forward P/E as a benchmark, FTEC trades at a moderate premium (therefore fair) compared to the historical distribution of relative valuations.

On the negative side, I admit that this is clearly due solely and exclusively to outsized EPS growth expectations, driven by the expansion of AI Capex over the past year. So on one hand valuations, taken on their own, in my view, communicate positivity, on the other hand, there remains the fear of a downward revision of guidance.

So … let’s try to look at some target prices. But first…

What to know about FTEC

FTEC is an indexed ETF, therefore passively managed, that replicates the MSCI USA IMI Information Technology 25/50 Index with a TER of 0.084%, a Bid/ask spread (30 days) of 0.04%, and a truly minimal Portfolio turnover, at 9%. All of this is nevertheless well covered by a 30-day SEC yield of 0.39%, for a Distribution yield (TTM) of 0.42%.

Holdings

With around 290 securities, it is one of the (apparently) diversified thematic ETFs. But the illusion of diversification quickly fades when we look at how market cap is distributed. Here as well, the top 10 holdings account for about 59% of the weight, and narrowing down to the top 3, as much as 44%.

Entirely US-centric and info-tech–centric, where large caps account for up to 76%, considering that more than 31% of the fund is composed solely of semiconductor stocks: NVDA alone weighs about 17% of FTEC.

Peers

Taking XLK as a reference, there is no major divergence in terms of performance.

One could say that the slight positive contribution that FTEC brings compared to XLK is due to a higher number of holdings, but the truth is that this “breadth” then loses significance when we notice that the top 3 account for 44% of the fund.

In a market where new instruments are continuously being created that introduce filters in the research process, such as factors or true active management, the real comparison for FTEC should, in my opinion, go elsewhere. For example, this peer table that includes an “equal weight” and a momentum strategy (two often discussed alternatives).

The truth is that when the market is highly liquid and not very fragmented, like the technology market, indexed ETFs remain unbeatable, especially when structured as market-weighted. In this case, FTEC wins.

My opinion

What scares investors who are approaching the Information Technology sector today? The forward P/E of 26x, still above the S&P 500 average of 22x. But I start from an assumption: is the info tech sector, and therefore FTEC, really that expensive? In reality, if we look at the distribution of past P/E ratios, today it stands at the 74th percentile of the 10-year distribution and at the 72nd percentile of the 30-year one. Sure, there is a valuation premium, but apparently it is moderate in forward terms, not as extreme as financial headlines describe it. The reason? FactSet gives us an answer. Expected earnings growth - CY 2026 (YoY, FactSet) is today close to 29% (the highest among all sectors); revenue growth is also expected at 17/18%. This makes one think: everyone expects a tech bubble, but it is neither the most expensive in relative terms, nor the least promising in prospective terms.

In fact, it is one of the few sectors that since December 31 has seen an increase in EPS guidance, without showing capital appreciation (indeed, a pullback).

Moreover, be careful: market multiple expansion is not the only determinant that justifies the increase in the performance of S&P 500 stocks. Academic studies, such as those proposed by Carson, show that multiples explain less than 28% of S&P 500 growth. The rest comes from revenues/earnings and dividends, and it so happens that expectations point to a record expansion, seen only in 2018 and indeed in the 2000s (the dot-com bubble).

Risk

There are certainly risks: what are they? A possible excess in the valuation of returns derived from AI investments, in my view, must be placed first. A growth slowdown would push multiples to extreme levels, and the market would likely apply a “mean reversion” mechanism to prices.

A possible cause:

Indeed, there is a high concentration in a few names in FTEC; essentially NVDA, Apple, and Microsoft, focusing on NVDA and Microsoft, consider that Apple is effectively an outsider in the AI race. Both have above-average valuations, graded “red” by SA. And what could therefore negatively impact these valuations?

Simply a deceleration in growth would be enough. And assuming that AI monetization estimates are coherent, indirectly I might have some concerns about interest rates.

NVDA and MSFT are long-duration stocks, and, as we all know well, a large part of their value lies in future cash flows (over 10 years). This makes the valuations of these companies quite sensitive to expectations on real rates (because in the end this directly affects the discount rate).

The doubt comes from the market: it does not seem to be so aligned with political expectations of rate cuts, Powell or not. This can be seen on Treasury maturities beyond 10 years: Treasury yields are rising, influenced by real rates (it is clear on the 30Y). This weighs on the discount rate, in my view.

A potential negative target

Assuming a mean reversion in expected earnings, which from 29% might drop to 14%, like those expected for the S&P 500 in 2026. What would happen? The forward P/E would become 54x, higher than the dot-com bubble, and in my opinion, unsustainable for the market. Assuming that 26x is an adequate premium for the market, we would be talking about a 48% decline according to my calculations, which, by forcing the application of this calculation to FTEC, would imply a price of around $125. (I repeat, this is a personal estimate, not an official target.)

A decline that would, however, be out of scale compared to the common risk measures shared on FTEC by SA. In this sense, FTEC has a standard deviation of 20.72 and an annualized volatility of 28.6%. Therefore, a 48% crash would be a “financial crisis” or “bubble” type crash, not a repricing.

Conclusion

I would like to remind everyone that these considerations are the result of my own rework, so I would really like a good discussion to arise in the comments about them. That said, I won’t deny that in my opinion, at least based on current data, I think the right rating for FTEC is BUY, precisely in order to follow the data-dependent nature that has guided my assessments on info tech up to today. At the same time, I acknowledge that the risk of having overestimated the returns from AI CAPEX is not negligible. And if this were to materialize, downside targets would not be just around the corner.

data.title: NEA: Really Sweet Return, Now Manage Expectations (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Main Thesis & Background

The purpose of this article is to evaluate the Nuveen AMT-Free Quality Muni (NEA) as an investment option at its current market price. This is a multi-state, closed-end fund with an objective "to provide current income exempt from regular federal income tax and the alternative minimum tax applicable to individuals by investing in an actively managed portfolio of tax-exempt municipal securities".

My followers know I have covered NEA for years, and I have a pretty good track record on it. I don't always rate this fund a buy - I never pump any investment endlessly - but I have found that timing this when the valuation is attractive has worked out very well over time. That was true back in mid-2025 when I suggested investors consider this CEF. Looking back, it has delivered a double-digit return that actually mirrors the equity market:

As you can see, NEA has been a winner of late. But with 2026 underway and volatility heating back up, it got me thinking I should evaluate this fund again to see if a rating change was warranted.

After review, I still think NEA is a strong long-term option, and I think the backdrop for munis here is supported. But I question if now is really the best time to buy. The fund's discount (which I viewed favorably last year) has evaporated, and the market is telling me that getting a bit more discerning with entry points now is important. With this understanding, I am shifting my rating to "hold" and will explain why in greater detail below.

The Valuation Story Has Shifted

One of my primary reasons for changing my outlook here has to do with valuation, as I alluded to in the opening paragraph. This is a fairly straightforward discussion, but given its importance, I want to highlight it first. Simply - NEA is not as cheap as it was. And I don't mean the market price because that is obvious given the 12% rise. But back in June I saw the 5% discount to NAV as an attractive time to get in. Today, that discount is gone, and the fund sits with a slight premium above its underlying value:

The takeaway here is NEA is no longer "cheap". This is slightly expected given the valuation story across the market universe, combined with NEA's own 12% gain (plus distributions). I wouldn't really anticipate it being a bargain after all that. But given that reality, it is hard for me to get excited about opening up any new positions at this level, and that suggests to me that prudence makes sense.

Yield Advantage Clear For Highly Taxed

I will now shift to the why behind why someone would want to be considering munis at all right now. This is relevant for NEA but also for any individual muni play or fund. There are two fundamental reasons I think munis still make sense to hold - and potentially buy under the right circumstances - in this environment.

This first has to do with yield. When we compare munis against treasuries and IG-rated corporate bonds, the value proposition is clear. This is true, however, for investors in higher tax brackets. For those not subject to federal (or state) income taxes and/or have a low tax burden, this story won't be the same. This is why it is so essential to consider your own unique situation when determining if munis are right for you.

But, if they are, it should be easy to see why. The after-tax yield for munis is far and away a higher income stream right now compared to comparable sectors:

The conclusion I draw here is that if one is willing to take on only a small amount of credit risk - which I consider to be the case for all three sectors (munis, IG corporates, and treasuries)—then munis are a reasonable play. With interest rates falling, a yield in the 6% range for the sector is appealing in my view. This tells me to keep munis at the forefront of my mind right now, especially as equity markets are getting rattled.

Equities Look Expensive

As noted in the final sentence of the prior paragraph, equities are starting to see an uptick in volatility, and this combines with a relatively expensive backdrop across the board. While many countries / economic zones are cheaper than others, it should be clear that P/E ratios are elevated broadly:

This graphic is showing that, across the board, forward P/Es are higher than their long-term average. This should make investors cautious - and it certainly is making me so!

The relevance here is we do not invest in a vacuum. I typically look to equity hedges when equities are looking pricey, and that is true right now for sure. For me, that includes gold, silver, and - of course - munis. This doesn't automatically make NEA the right investment, but it does underscore why I am even considering munis at all at this time.

Transportation Bonds Supported By Cash

Let's look into NEA in particular now. One aspect about this fund that catches my eye at the moment is its exposure to Transportation securities. This sub-sector of the muni market makes up just over 15% of total fund assets, so its impact on the fund is significant:

Fortunately, this is an area I think has a favorable outlook for 2026. I support this view because these are bonds supported by what is perhaps the most important thing - cash. If we look at common transportation securities, which include toll roads/bridges and airports, we see that metrics are strong:

What this graphic shows is that issuers of this type of debt have plenty of cash on hand, and their coverage ratio has been high and growing. That is a win-win in my view, and I see these securities as partially responsible for NEA's gain of late. There is a chance this could continue, and that is why I would be reluctant to place anything less than "hold" on this fund for now.

Distribution Remains A Thorn

My final point is just another word of caution on NEA in isolation. I mentioned valuation early on, but there is a second reason I am less bullish on this fund for the months ahead. This is in regard to the fund's distribution. It continues to be "high", but also very reliant on return of capital. This means a cut is a continual looming headwind, and that is something to consider when evaluating the risk:

The premise here is I don't love when CEFs overpay beyond a reasonable level. For me, breaching the 40% threshold is a bit unreasonable. This may help entice investors in the short run, but over the long term, it can be destructive to the NAV and can set up a sharp decline in share price if the fund ultimately does cut the distribution and the market reacts negatively to it.

In sum, I don't view NEA's exceptionally high yield as a positive attribute right now. I see it ripe for a correction, and that will pressure the share price in all likelihood - especially given the run it has had of late. This makes me a bit more cautious in early 2026 than I was last year.

Expense Ratio Could Push Lower

My final thought is in regard to the fund's expense ratio. On the surface, those new to these types of funds may find 3% as a management and leverage fee to be high. I would argue that this is actually very reasonable given the amount of leverage the fund holds (which is over 40%). Readers should remember this is borrowing that can amplify a fund's returns (in both directions) and is a cheaper way to borrow than what we as retail traders could find.

Further, the Fed has embarked on a rate-cutting cycle, and that is likely to continue in 2026. This could have an impact on shorter term borrowing rates and offer even better terms for leverage borrowing by funds like NEA in the year ahead. The relevance is that I don't view NEA to be too "expensive" in terms of what management is charging to own it, and I expect the cost for investors will actually drift lower along with the interest rate environment. The conclusion is not to be put off by this aspect of the fund and consider that it may actually be a favorable attribute compared to other CEFs that have a higher expense structure.

Bottom Line

NEA has had a great run, and I hope everyone who has owned it since mid-2025 has patted themselves on the back! Looking ahead, there is the potential for more gains to be sure. The fund's holdings are well supported, and the broader muni sector could see a push higher as investors rotate out of equities given the current climate.

But risks are present as well. The fund's distribution remains heavily reliant on return of capital, and the valuation is no longer as compelling as it was six months ago. In short, I think readers would be wise to manage expectations here and be patient, as a better opportunity is likely to present itself in the coming months. I am therefore putting a "hold" rating on this CEF and suggest to my followers they approach any new positions selectively going forward.

data.title: U.S. GDP Beats And Monday Gaps Fill: Dow Jones And U.S. Stock Index Outlook
data.description:
By Elior Manier

This morning brought a fresh wave of optimism to Wall Street. First, traders received an update from the Trump Administration regarding Greenland. The recent rhetoric had posed significant sentiment hurdles, triggering repricings after a positive start to 2026.

The situation shifted yesterday afternoon when Trump posted that a framework for Greenland's strategic use by the US had been reached. While details remain unclear, the tone is calmer, easing fears of a NATO collapse or armed invasion. However, Greenland will likely continue to assert its sovereignty, meaning tensions could flare if the deal does not align with local views.

Combined with the latest data, US sentiment is back at highs. Q3 GDP beat expectations (4.4% vs 4.3% expected), arriving alongside slight misses in PCE data. This combination supports a soft-landing scenario, the most favorable outcome for stocks.

The ongoing earnings season is also leaving a decent impression. The key Magnificent 7 reports still face the test of extreme expectations, but the path should become clearer ahead of the January 28 FOMC meeting.

Microsoft (MSFT), Meta (META), Tesla (TSLA), and ASML (ASML) report after the close on FOMC day, while Apple (AAPL) and Visa (V) follow on Thursday.

The picture is once again very green all around the market, with Meta leading to the upside (+4.22%) and Mag 7s sustaining well after their more volatile 2025 year-end. This expresses some repositioning ahead of their earnings next week. Energy minerals and producer manufacturing are among the lagging sectors.

Dive into our daily session charts and trading levels for the major US indexes: Dow Jones, Nasdaq, and S&P 500. Watch for pre-weekend volatility as the situation is not fully clear yet, and Iran remains a factor.

Dow Jones 4H Chart

Now easily brushing back above the 49,000 level, the DJIA is testing its all-time highs. The Morning 4H Candle just closed and expresses some form of momentum slowdown at previous highs, confirming more rangebound price action that could be expected all the way to next week's FOMC.

  • Breaking and closing above the 49,715 all-time highs could see follow through as long as a break occurs on strong volume. Momentum has gotten more bullish, but the TACO spike could slow down.
  • A pullback here could easily test the 49,150 level, which crosses with the 4H 50-period MA
  • Any session close below the 49,000 key level brings back seller strength, which could move towards the 4H 200-MA (48,620)

Dow Jones technical levels for trading:

Resistance Levels

  • Session Highs: 49,628
  • 49,650 to 49,670 Current ATH Resistance
  • All-time highs: 49,710
  • 50,000 Potential Psychological Resistance

Support Levels

  • 49,150 4H 50-period MA
  • Christmas ATH High Timeframe Momentum Pivot – 49,000
  • Intraday support 48,600 to 48,700
  • November ATH 48,300 to 48,500 Mini-Support
  • Psychological Support at 48,000
  • 45,000 psychological level (Main Support on higher timeframe)

Nasdaq 4H Chart

Nasdaq is once again stuck in its higher timeframe range (24,500 to 25,800) which has been containing its progress since its October drop. The two-session rebound is still very impressive, with the index up 2.50% during that span.

Looking further to higher timeframes, consolidations at relative all-time highs are a positive sign as it allows overbought conditions from the previous year to largely ease – conditions needed for clean breakouts.

Still, bulls will have to manage a daily close above the 25,700 resistance in order to generate further momentum and potentially push for a new all-time high record.

Next week's earnings will have a huge influence on stock movement. Keep a close attention to the Fed's communications next week.

Nasdaq technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Session Highs: 25,625
  • Intermediate Resistance 25,700 to 25,850
  • All-time high resistance zone 26,100 to 26,300
  • Current ATH: 26,182

Support Levels

  • Momentum Pivot 25,200 to 25,500 +/- 75 pts
  • Session lows: 25,425
  • Minor Support: 25,000 to 25,250
  • 24,500 Main support
  • Early 2025 ATH at 22,000 to 22,229 Support

S&P 500 4H Chart

The S&P 500 is about 100 points higher compared to our past-day analysis, but the index is expressing some technical doubts as it faces a retest of its Monday futures gap (6,930).

The current dragonfly doji candle indicates decent bull resilience but generally balanced action, which could easily lead to a retest of the 6,880 to 6,900 pivot area.

For bull/bear indications, traders can spot whether the action remains above the 4H 50-period MA (6,915) – closing below on high volume can prompt a further correction to 6,830-6,850, past day support levels.

S&P 500 technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Session highs: 6,938
  • Previous ATH Resistance 6,945 to 6,975
  • Current ATH resistance at 7,000

Support Levels

  • Pivot Zone 6,880 to 6,900
  • Mini-Support 6,830 to 6,850 (Past day support)
  • 6,800 Psychological Support
  • 6,789 session lows
  • Support 6,720 to 6,750 (mid-December lows at 6,729)
  • 6,400 major psychological support

Don't forget to stay close to the headlines, as the World Economic Forum is still ongoing!

Safe Trades!

Original Post

data.title: Core PCE Inflation At 2.8% In November, As Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Fed’s preferred inflation gauge, the core PCE price index, rose 2.8% year-over-year in November. This was consistent with the forecast and a slight pickup from October's 2.7% reading. On a monthly basis, the core index was up 0.2%, as expected.

The headline index was up 2.8% year-over-year, up from 2.7% in October and in line with the forecast. On a monthly basis, the index was up 0.2%, as expected.

PCE Price Index

Personal consumption expenditures (PCE) measures and tracks changes for all domestic personal consumption. Core PCE measures the changes in personal consumption less food and energy, making it less volatile than the headline PCE. The PCE Price Index is calculated using PCE data and is a key way to measure changes in purchasing trends and inflation.

The adjacent thumbnail gives us a close-up of the trend in YoY core PCE since January 2012. The first string of red data points highlights the 12 consecutive months when core PCE hovered in a narrow range around its interim low.

The second string highlights the lower range from late 2014 through 2015. Core PCE shifted higher in 2016 with a decline in 2017, 2019, and 2020, with a major jump in 2022.

The following chart shows the monthly year-over-year change in the Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) price index since 2000. An overlay of the core PCE price index is also included, with the highlighted line on the chart representing the 2% benchmark for core inflation.

For a long-term perspective, here are the same two metrics spanning five decades.

Inflation: Fed's 2% Target

The Fed traditionally uses the PCE Price Index as their preferred inflation gauge; however, another common measure of inflation is the Consumer Price Index.

In their latest meeting, the Fed implemented a widely anticipated 25 basis point cut to the federal funds rate (FFR), bringing it to a range of 3.50%-3.75%. This is the third consecutive rate cut and puts the central bank’s range at its lowest level since November 2022.

The statement from the meeting revealed the committee believes "inflation has moved up since earlier in the year and remains somewhat elevated," but that they are strongly committed to returning inflation to its 2% objective.

The Fed will meet again next week, where they are expected to hold rates steady. At the time of writing, the CME FedWatch Tool shows a 95% likelihood the Fed will hold rates where they are versus a 5% likelihood of a 25 basis point reduction.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Back To Fundamentals: 3 Resilient Picks For 2026
data.description:
Last year, I wrote an article titled "Back to fundamentals: 3 Picks for 2025". The picks were Nvidia (NVDA), PDD Holdings (PDD), and Pinterest (PINS). You can tell simply by those picks that my perspective going into the year surrounded Growth At a Reasonable Price (GARP), without much respect to risk or sleep-well-at-night (SAWN) status. Feel free to read the article and its reasoning. While the picks have fared decently (as an average), I'll personally be waiting 3-5 years to judge the performance of these companies and my call.

I'd like to select a new set of companies for 2026. I'll admit I have become less adventurous, having actually sold both my PDD and Pinterest holdings at reasonable gain through the year, despite truly enjoying being a shareholder and still believing in their business model and potential. Frankly, both companies continue to deserve higher valuations. For example, PDD's stock is $106, with $58B in net cash. This leads to a forward EV/EBIT of 6.8x and nearly $6B annually simply from interest payments. I could go on. As for Pinterest, the company grew revenues 16.8% in the last quarter and is trading at 13.1x forward earnings, with a large net cash position and a user base growing at 12% annually in monthly actives. These numbers continue to not make much sense, which is why we should be giving the market time to figure out the value here or for the companies to produce the earnings I expect they will.

Regardless, here are my top picks for 2026, with a strong emphasis on moats.

Meta Platforms (META)

Meta Platforms was a laggard in 2025 and has fallen through 2026.

I've written about Meta before, highlighting its several social media platforms, its monetization of Threads and WhatsApp, and its budding smart glasses business, through which the company expects to deliver Personal Superintelligence For All. You can review that in-depth article here.

  • Meta Platforms: Future Bright in Several Spots, Valuation More Than Acceptable for Long-Term Shareholders

Today, I want to focus on two key reasons Meta Platforms remains a Strong Buy for me.

1. Accelerating Business

Given what I've covered in previous articles, this business has extremely strong fundamentals. The company's main business metric, its Family of Apps Monthly Active Users (MAU), grew 7.6% year over year in the last quarter, accelerating from 6.4% annually from the quarter before.

This culminated in revenue growth of 26.25%. If nothing else, you can hang onto that as a huge lever in favor of Meta shareholders. No other companies in the Magnificent 7 are growing like that, except Nvidia (NVDA). The law of large numbers clearly applies at much larger numbers than Meta's current revenue figures.

The company did take a huge one-time tax charge anticipating the effects of the One Big Beautiful Bill, but the market didn't seem to react to that upon the earnings release. Instead, the market has been fixated on Meta's lagging AI competitiveness. Meta has been very slow getting its AI MAUs and their traffic volume up to levels seen at Google.

Regardless, Meta's business is social media, not AI - and in that regard, 26% revenue growth is astounding.

2. Valuation

This bull market has been expensive since it was born in late October 2022. As such, one would expect Meta to be valued at least in line with the market and/or its megacap peers.

Instead, the company is trading at 19.9x 2026 earnings, or if you prefer operating cash flow, Meta is trading at 12x 2026 OCF. Note below that Meta's long-term operating cash flow multiple is over 17x. A reversion to trend here by 2027 would get us to a price target of $1067, counting dividends. This does not count any AI progress the company is actively attempting.

Risks

An ordinary recession is always a risk for Meta's shareholders in the short term. Companies of all sizes cut their ad budgets, directly impacting Meta Platforms. Yet, had you bought Meta at its September 2021 peak before the 2022 bear market, you'd have returned 12% annually for 4 years, slightly outperforming the S&P 500.

One could also argue Meta is at risk of disruption from AI. Theoretically, social media users could get AI to auto-generate content. Obviously I don't buy this for a minute - social media is social in nature - comments sections, drama, niche interests, and your personal friends group are why I believe users continue to grow at Meta's platforms. I'm going to go as far as coining the term "cultural media" which more appropriately describes what happens on "social" media, while also indicating Meta's significance and embeddedness in the world.

Mastercard (MA)

Meta Platforms is my growth play idea for 2026 because the market has truly discounted its growth without AI, let alone what could be possible with AI (such as embedded in their smart glasses).

As a second idea for "secured growth", in other words, growth that is risk-adjusted, investors can hardly do better than Mastercard at current levels. Mastercard is the world's second-largest credit card company, beaten only by Visa (V). The company supports its merchants with a very strong and budding software side business called Value-Added Services and Solutions. Within this business line, which Mastercard has been cross-selling to its merchants, growth has been a steady 22-25% annually.

Importantly, Mastercard carries no debt associated with its cardholders - the banks carry such debt. Mastercard simply connects the cardholder's bank to the merchant's bank through quite a complex and irreplicable payments network.

Valuation

While Mastercard has never really been dirt cheap, its valuation recently has become enticing, especially in the face of all-time stock market highs and several international pressures and drama we're all already aware of.

Apart from the bear market of 2022, Mastercard has not been this cheap since 2017. Better still, the company comes with little debt given its worldwide embeddedness, standing at 3.5x long-term debt/EBITDA, and the company is one of the most widely held companies among super-investors.

A reversion to trend by the end of 2027 would get us to a share price of $875, counting dividends and assuming 16% OCF growth.

Risks

The company's risks mainly include greater regulatory oversight or a consumer-led recession. Both such risks are par for the course for almost any publicly traded company.

Waste Connections (WCN)

My risk aversion from 2025 has amplified into 2026. To be frank, I don't care how well amazing opportunities in little-known companies do - I know I'll be uncomfortable holding them if I don't feel they are monumental and important companies (one of my Five Factors). Further, I've decided to assume risks I've been worried about will indeed happen and adjust my portfolio as such, instead of hoping they don't. Therefore, I expect a Chinese blockade of Taiwan in the coming five years, something I covered in detail in my October article on Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), below.

  • Three Serious Problems Owning Taiwan Semiconductor

Further, as mentioned previously, indices are at or near all-time highs, with the (SP500) quite far above its long-term averages.

As such, I believe it's totally reasonable and prudent to own something uncorrelated with all the following three issues: a problem in Taiwan, a consumer or economic recession, and a market multiple contraction. The company that perhaps most diversifies away from these risks while maintaining more than reasonable growth is Waste Connections.

In short, Waste Connections has long-term contracts with municipalities to pick up their garbage and manage it at Waste Connections' landfills. The company has expanded into methane collection from their landfills. It competes mostly in a duopoly with Waste Management (WM), but in reality Waste Management focuses on urban centers, while Waste Connections focuses on rural areas. Both companies are rather insulated from each other, in that establishing a second landfill in a given area will always face the epitome of NIMBYism, while establishing the first one is welcomed by the municipality.

Further, Waste Connections maintains a slightly different business model. In rural areas, the cost per pickup is larger, given the greater distances the garbage trucks must travel, while in urban areas the cost per pickup is less due to urban density. As such, Waste Management encroaching on Waste Connection's rural areas would be a double negative effect of lowering margins while attempting to establish alternative landfills to Waste Connections, something no one wants. As such, Waste Connections' growth has gone relatively unhindered.

Risks

Waste Connections will likely always be here across North America to pick up our garbage, and the chance of disruption from AI or municipal contract failure due to recessions is about zero. I specifically bought this company's shares recently because I could hardly conceive of any risks. I believe Waste Connections largest risk is either mismanagement, something any company faces, or valuation concerns.

Valuation

Waste Connections has been historically valued around 36x adjusted earnings. At the outset, this will be too rich for many value-based investors. However, when you consider the indestructibility of the company in the long term, a valuation like this, which mirrors valuations for Mastercard, begins to make sense. Further, it's somewhat helpful to know that the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation Trust - a charity whose investments are designed to survive and grow long-term—maintains a 1% position in WCN and a whopping 17% position in WM, a strong nod to the industry's long-term survivability.

A reversion to historical valuations, which can likely be expected given the lack of AI threats, would push the stock price to $354, including dividends, a fantastic return on a risk-adjusted basis.

Conclusion

As I've pivoted from growth to risk-adjusted growth in the last 7 or 8 months, my perspectives on my portfolio have evolved in unexpected ways. This has led me from a total return-at-all-costs mentality to a risk-adjusted total return mentality.

Consequently, I've been prioritizing these three companies, along with two more I hope to write about in the coming weeks, in order to capture adequate growth for my portfolio while mitigating risks I see in the current environment, which I really dislike.

The combination of Meta Platforms, Mastercard, and Waste Connections casts a wide net across tollbooth-like companies with entrenched businesses whose industries face massive barriers to entry, where AI is of no use in breaking down those barriers. I'm rating all these companies a Strong Buy, indicating I have purchased or added to all three in the last few days. These three companies alone sum to over 30% of my portfolio, and I may intend to purchase more should valuations allow it.

data.title: European Wax Center: Despite The Pain, This Prospect Offers Upside
data.description:
Even though I have established for myself a good track record over the long run, I'm not going to sit here and say that my timing is always right. A good example of my timing not playing out the way that I thought it would involves European Wax Center, Inc. (EWCZ), a rather interesting business that focuses on operating locations dedicated to providing waxing services. Back in July of last year, I decided to upgrade the stock from a "Hold" to a "Buy." This was driven by improved clarity when it came to future growth and robust cash flows that the company was exhibiting. Since that time, however, the market has disagreed with me. Shares have been pushed lower to the tune of 19.8%. This is at a time when the S&P 500 is up 8.9%.

As painful as this is to see, I believe the fundamentals are working in my favor. Yes, revenue is still dropping, but bottom-line results have improved quite a bit. We are also seeing some improvements in same store sales, which is excellent. And when it comes to the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety, management projected that profits would be higher than they were the previous year. Add on top of this just how cheap shares are, and I do think that maintaining it as a "Buy" candidate is the right move.

This Hurt

Just like waxing sometimes does, or at least from what I've been told since I have never had it done personally, European Wax Center has experienced some pain as of late. Take the most recent quarter as an example. This involves the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. According to management, revenue amounted to $54.2 million. This was actually down from the $55.4 million the company reported a year earlier. Unfortunately, system-wide sales contracted slightly from $240.2 million to $238.2 million. This came as the number of locations in operation pulled back from 1,064 to 1,053.

This drop was intentional. Management has acknowledged that the company is currently in the early stages of a transformation aimed at improving operations. While the company still believes in long-term growth prospects, it blames a weak consumer environment for its troubles. Many franchisees ended up pausing their new center growth plans as the company sought to stabilize operations. And because of declining transactions and profitability, the company has watched as the number of locations has dropped. In fact, for 2025 in its entirety, the company told investors that they would close between 35 and 40 locations. That compares to just 12 openings. The good news, however, is that the closing count actually marks a big improvement from the 40 to 60 locations that the company previously anticipated closing for the year.

Normally when revenue drops, profitability also takes a hit. But that's not what we saw here. In fact, net income jumped from $1.5 million to $3.8 million. Some of this was because of a decline in advertising revenue. But the bigger impact involved a decline in selling, general, and administrative costs. Management chalked this up to lower payroll and benefits costs, a decrease in professional fees, a reduction in corporate marketing, and lower provisions for bad debts. There was also a decline in recruiting costs. All of these are good signs in the sense that they show that management is making progress on transforming the business so that it can thrive in the long run. The fact that same-center sales actually increased by 0.2% year over year also helped, since it means more revenue dollars coming in relative to fixed costs. That is definitely friendly from a margin standpoint.

Other profitability metrics have also improved. Adjusted net income, for instance, grew from $9.4 million to $10.7 million. Operating cash flow popped from $14.9 million to $17.3 million. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $11.6 million to $14.2 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company increased from $18.4 million to $20.2 million. All of this is great to see, and what's even better is that this was not a blip on the radar. In the chart below, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. Revenue declined once again. But profits and every single cash flow metric for the company improved.

Management said that system-wide sales for the 2025 fiscal year as a whole would likely be somewhere between $945 million and $948 million. To put that in perspective, for 2024, they amounted to $951.3 million. That's not a horrible decline, but it's a drop all the same. And as a result, management expects that revenue will have dropped from $216.9 million to between $206 million and $208 million. This is even though same-center revenue is expected to inch up by between 0.1% and 0.3%, with the drop actually being attributable to the aforementioned decline in location count.

Management's own guidance called for EBITDA to range between $72 million and $74 million for the year. They also said that adjusted net profits would be somewhere between $33 million and $35 million. And if we project out results based on what we have seen already for 2025, we would likely get adjusted operating cash flow of somewhere in the range of $59.3 million. For a company, this size, with a market capitalization of $214.7 million and an enterprise value of $539.8 million, this is a lot in the way of profitability.

When it comes to valuing the company, comparative analysis is not really feasible. There aren't any good comparable firms to stack up against this. The closest you could get would be beauty-related companies, but even those have some major differences. Because of this, I think that just analyzing the valuation of the company in a vacuum is more appropriate. Doing this, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. Honestly, these are really low multiples. And even though financial performance has been mixed as of late, the firm still has a net leverage ratio of about 4.09. That's not great, but it's not so high that I am concerned. That's especially in light of the improvements the company has seen on the bottom line, with management working to transform the business.

When it comes to 2026, we don't have any detailed guidance. However, the company has acknowledged that it is facing continuing challenges when it comes to new guest acquisition and when it comes to certain regional markets that it operates in. This means that management still has progress that needs to be made. Having said that, the company does expect to achieve a return to unit growth by the end of this year, which, when combined with management's continued push to improve profitability by cutting costs, does create an opportunity for some meaningful upside in the second half of this year.

Takeaway

Fundamentally speaking, European Wax Center might not be the best prospect out there. But it is an interesting value play that I view as a turnaround candidate. The improvements in profitability have been immensely reassuring, though what we really need to see in order for massive upside to be on the table is a return to revenue growth as well. For now, I think that maintaining it as a soft "Buy" makes the most sense. But if we start to see some meaningful improvements, an upgrade could be in store.
